posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
A Friday puzzler:

Les Perrières seems to be one of the most common used lieu-dit/climat names – I can’t think of any names that appears more frequently in all of French wine. Meursault-Perrieres is the most well known, but there are also Perrieres in Puligny, NSG, Gevrey, Corton, and tons across the Maconnais and Chalonnaise. Tons out in the Loire as well, across Saumur, Vouvray, Sancerre, Savennieres, Bourgeuil, etc. Les Perrieres de Lafleur, too.

What’s with the name? According to the interwebs, Les Perrières is translated as “The Mortars”. That’s all well and good, but can anyone explain why it was used everywhere?

And whether deservedly or not, I also tend to associate the name with high quality, due to Meursault-Perrieres and Catherine Breton’s Bourgeuil. For example, when exploring random Macon whites, I unconsciously gravitate towards the name. Does this happen to others as well?

(Apologies for the clickbait-y title!! )

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: A good question. I have no answers but similarly associate it with good quality.

posts.name: Jim Petrila
posts.cooked:
Found this with a little internet searching:

This word from Old French – from late Latin PETRARIUM “stone quarry” derived from Latin PETRA “rock, boulder, stone” – designates a quarry, where stones and rocks were extracted for building dwellings and edifices. In the Burgundian vineyard toponymy, this word is found more often than the term Carrière (quarry) to designate the same reality. The viticultural Côte is the land of stone. Open cast quarries are still in activity here and there at Comblanchien or Chassagne-Montrachet, for example But most have been filled in to become vineyards. That is why climats and lieux-dits whose names are formed on Perrière point us to their previous existence. However, some of them were not quarries, but lands close to ancient metaled roads. This just shows the richness of the place name and its multiple meanings.
ATTENTION : copyright application iOS ClimaVinea or https://www.athenaeum.com/livre/2337006-climats-et-lieux-dits-des-grands-vignobles-de-b–sylvain-pitiot-marie-helene-landrieu-lussigny-editions-jean-pierre-de-monza (ISBN 978-2-916231-20-4)

I’m sure we will leave no stone unturned in researching this…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Found this with a little internet searching:

This word from Old French – from late Latin PETRARIUM “stone quarry” derived from Latin PETRA “rock, boulder, stone” – designates a quarry, where stones and rocks were extracted for building dwellings and edifices. In the Burgundian vineyard toponymy, this word is found more often than the term Carrière (quarry) to designate the same reality. The viticultural Côte is the land of stone. Open cast quarries are still in activity here and there at Comblanchien or Chassagne-Montrachet, for example But most have been filled in to become vineyards. That is why climats and lieux-dits whose names are formed on Perrière point us to their previous existence. However, some of them were not quarries, but lands close to ancient metaled roads. This just shows the richness of the place name and its multiple meanings.
ATTENTION : copyright application iOS ClimaVinea or https://www.athenaeum.com/livre/2337006-climats-et-lieux-dits-des-grands-vignobles-de-b–sylvain-pitiot-marie-helene-landrieu-lussigny-editions-jean-pierre-de-monza (ISBN 978-2-916231-20-4)

This is the right answer! These sites were ancient quarries for stone. The parcel of Meursault Perrières belonging to Pierre and Anne Morey, for example, is where the shards of stone left over from quarrying were discarded. And here, in Nuits-Saint-Georges, you can see the only very recently retired quarry in Les Perrières: Google Maps

Obviously, these sites are indeed generally stony…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I believe Pierrières translates to stones. Don’t know what Perrières means in English.

“P**i**errières” = stony ground. Olivier Collin’s Pierrières, for example, comes from a place where there are a lot of flints embedded in the chalk. But you do not see this name very often.

But that’s nothing to do with Perrières, meaning quarry.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: The person who said mortar is also right. This is a medieval word for catapult that also has an obsolete regional meaning of quarry–hence William’s stony ground. This is an internet dictionary definition, though. I don’t have my Petit Robert at hand to check.

posts.name: Eric Texier
posts.cooked: AFAIK, perrière is an old french word for quarry.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The person who said mortar is also right. This is a medieval word for catapult that also has an obsolete regional meaning of quarry–hence William’s stony ground. This is an internet dictionary definition, though. I don’t have my Petit Robert at hand to check.

My “stony ground” was in reference to the rare lieu-dit name Pierrierès, with the additional “i”, rather than Perrières.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Great! Ok, I’m guessing lots of limestone quarries historically in Burgundy and the Loire, so historically a popular name. Any thoughts on how often this relates to quality - and do other people have this perceived bias towards the name that Marcus and I share? And would this maybe be why say, Lafleur renamed Acte to Les Perrieres de Lafleur?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
And would this maybe be why say, Lafleur renamed Acte to Les Perrieres de Lafleur?

They always intended to call it that, when (if) the wine had a sufficiently mature and individual expression to merit it! It derives from the limestone plateau of Fronsac, outside the AOC, and the vineyard (like many in Saint-Emilion and elsewhere) sits on top of big tunnels created in the quarrying of limestone to build the city of Bordeaux. So it isn’t a fanciful name!

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
The names for almost all Burgundy vineyards come from rudimentary names given them by the Cistercians to distinguish one vineyard from another. Whatever the name, they almost surely sound better in French. The stones; the chalk, the crow; the
Good sticks; and others sound exotic- in French.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
